description: Add browser tools as a first-class section in the Chrome extension side panel, matching the existing plugin card pattern with per-tool enable/disable toggles and full Storybook coverage.

userStories.title: Add shared wire types for browser tools in config.getState
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the config.getState wire protocol to include browser tool definitions so the side panel can display them.
acceptanceCriteria: ConfigStateBrowserTool interface exists in platform/shared/src/index.ts with fields: name (string), description (string), enabled (boolean)
ConfigStateResult interface includes a new browserTools: ConfigStateBrowserTool[] field
ConfigSetBrowserToolEnabledParams interface exists with fields: tool (string), enabled (boolean)
userStories.notes: Edit platform/shared/src/index.ts. Add ConfigStateBrowserTool near the existing ConfigStatePlugin (around line 244). Add browserTools field to ConfigStateResult (line 271). Add ConfigSetBrowserToolEnabledParams near ConfigSetToolEnabledParams (line 276). Browser tools are simpler than plugin tools — they don't have icons, displayName, iconSvg, etc. Just name, description, and enabled. The name field is the full browser tool name like 'browser_list_tabs'. Export all new types.

userStories.title: Extend config.getState handler to include browser tools
userStories.description: As the side panel, I want config.getState to return the list of browser tools with their enabled state so I can render them.
acceptanceCriteria: handleConfigGetState in extension-handlers.ts includes browserTools array in the result
Each browser tool entry has name (from CachedBrowserTool.name), description (from CachedBrowserTool.description), and enabled (from isBrowserToolEnabled)
Browser tools are sorted alphabetically by name
userStories.notes: Edit platform/mcp-server/src/extension-handlers.ts, specifically handleConfigGetState (around line 212). After building the plugins array, build a browserTools array by mapping state.cachedBrowserTools: { name: ct.name, description: ct.description, enabled: isBrowserToolEnabled(state, ct.name) }. Sort by name. Include it in the result object alongside plugins and failedPlugins. Import isBrowserToolEnabled from './state.js' if not already imported. Also import ConfigStateBrowserTool from '@opentabs-dev/shared' for the type annotation. Add/update a test in extension-handlers.test.ts that verifies browserTools is present in the config.getState response.

userStories.title: Add config.setBrowserToolEnabled handler and persistence
userStories.description: As a side panel user, I want to toggle individual browser tools on/off so that I can control which browser tools are available to AI agents.
acceptanceCriteria: Validates the tool name exists in state.cachedBrowserTools
Persists to config.json via a new saveBrowserToolPolicy function in config.ts (read-modify-write pattern like saveToolConfig)
Calls callbacks.onToolConfigChanged() to notify MCP clients of the tool list change
Sends plugins.changed notification to extension so side panel refreshes
New McpCallbacks field onBrowserToolPolicyPersist for persistence (or reuse existing pattern)
Tests cover valid toggle, invalid tool name, missing params
userStories.notes:
This story touches multiple files in platform/mcp-server/src/:

1. extension-handlers.ts: Add handleConfigSetBrowserToolEnabled following the pattern of handleConfigSetToolEnabled (line 241). Instead of looking up a plugin, validate tool name against state.cachedBrowserTools.some(c => c.name === tool). Update state.browserToolPolicy[tool] = enabled. Call callbacks.onToolConfigChanged() (triggers MCP tools/list change notification). Need to persist — see below. Send plugins.changed notification so side panel re-fetches. Export the new handler.

2. config.ts: Add saveBrowserToolPolicy function following saveToolConfig pattern (line 327). It should read-modify-write config.json, updating only the browserToolPolicy field while preserving other fields. Signature: saveBrowserToolPolicy(state: { configWriteMutex: Promise<void> }, browserToolPolicy: Record<string, boolean>): Promise<void>.

3. extension-protocol.ts: Add routing for 'config.setBrowserToolEnabled' method (around line 402, after setAllToolsEnabled). Route to handleConfigSetBrowserToolEnabled with state, params, id, callbacks.

4. McpCallbacks interface (extension-handlers.ts line 31): Add onBrowserToolPolicyPersist: () => void callback. Wire it up in http-routes.ts createMcpCallbacks (line 52) to call saveBrowserToolPolicy(state, { ...state.browserToolPolicy }).

5. extension-handlers.test.ts: Add tests for the new handler. Follow existing test patterns for config.setToolEnabled.

Import ConfigSetBrowserToolEnabledParams from '@opentabs-dev/shared'.

userStories.title: Add BrowserToolsCard component to the side panel
userStories.description: As a user viewing the side panel, I want to see browser tools displayed as a built-in section so I can see what browser capabilities are available and toggle them on/off.
acceptanceCriteria: Uses the Accordion pattern from PluginCard — expandable card with header and tool list
Header shows a Globe icon (from lucide-react), 'Browser Tools' title, and a toggle-all Switch
Expanded content shows one ToolRow per browser tool with enable/disable Switch
Supports toolFilter prop for search filtering (same matchesTool pattern)
Supports activeTools prop for showing active tool execution indicators
Toggle calls a new setBrowserToolEnabled bridge function
Toggle-all calls setBrowserToolEnabled for each tool (no dedicated server endpoint needed)
Optimistic updates with rollback on failure (same pattern as PluginCard)
userStories.notes:
Create platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/BrowserToolsCard.tsx. Follow PluginCard.tsx (same directory) as the pattern.

Key differences from PluginCard:
- No PluginMenu (no update/remove for built-in tools)
- No version badge, no DEV badge, no SDK badge
- No tabState (browser tools are always available when connected)
- Uses Globe icon from lucide-react instead of PluginIcon
- The 'plugin name' for activeTools tracking is 'browser' (matching the audit plugin field)
- No PluginIcon — use a simple Globe icon with a distinctive style

Also add to platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/bridge.ts:
- A new type alias: export type BrowserToolState = ConfigStateBrowserTool (import from @opentabs-dev/shared)
- A new setBrowserToolEnabled(tool: string, enabled: boolean) function that sends config.setBrowserToolEnabled JSON-RPC request via sendRequest

The component should accept props: { tools: BrowserToolState[], activeTools: Set<string>, onToolsChange: (updater: (tools: BrowserToolState[]) => BrowserToolState[]) => void, toolFilter?: string }.

For tool display: browser tools don't have displayName or icon fields in the wire type. Generate a displayName by converting the tool name: remove the 'browser_' or 'extension_' or 'plugin_' prefix, replace underscores with spaces, title-case each word. For the icon, use a mapping of known prefixes to lucide icons (e.g., 'browser_' tools get a Globe icon, 'extension_' tools get a Puzzle icon). Or just use a generic wrench icon since ToolRow already handles missing icons with a Wrench fallback.

For ToolRow compatibility: ToolRow expects name, displayName, description, icon, enabled, active, onToggle. Create the displayName and icon from the browser tool name. Use 'globe' as the default icon for browser_ tools, 'puzzle' for extension_ tools, 'search' for plugin_analyze_site.

The BrowserToolsCard header should look like the retro theme: use AccordionPrimitive directly like PluginCard does. Show a Globe icon (size 32, styled similarly to PluginIcon), 'Browser Tools' text, a BUILT-IN badge (using Badge variant='default' size='sm'), ChevronDown, and the toggle-all Switch.

userStories.title: Wire BrowserToolsCard into App.tsx and the data flow
userStories.description: As a user, I want the browser tools section to appear in the side panel above the plugin list, always visible when connected.
acceptanceCriteria: App.tsx has browserTools state (from config.getState response)
BrowserToolsCard renders above the PluginList when connected and browserTools is non-empty
Browser tools section is visible in both normal view and search filtered view
Search filtering applies to browser tools (same matchesTool logic)
Browser tool active state tracked via activeTools Set (key format: 'browser:<toolName>')
plugins.changed notification triggers re-fetch which updates browser tools too
userStories.notes:
Edit platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/App.tsx:

1. Add state: const [browserTools, setBrowserTools] = useState<BrowserToolState[]>([]); (import BrowserToolState from bridge)

2. In loadPlugins (around where fetchConfigState is called), extract browserTools from the result: const result = await fetchConfigState(); setPlugins(result.plugins); setFailedPlugins(result.failedPlugins); setBrowserTools(result.browserTools ?? []);

3. Import BrowserToolsCard from './components/BrowserToolsCard.js'

4. Render BrowserToolsCard ABOVE PluginList in the main content area, inside the same Accordion or as a separate section. Since BrowserToolsCard uses its own Accordion.Item, it should be placed inside the same Accordion wrapper as the plugin list, or as a standalone section above the plugin list.

Actually, looking at PluginList.tsx more carefully — it wraps all PluginCards in an Accordion. BrowserToolsCard should either:
- Be rendered ABOVE the PluginList component as a standalone Accordion with one item, OR
- Be integrated into PluginList which renders it as the first item

The cleaner approach is to render BrowserToolsCard as a separate section above PluginList. Give it its own Accordion wrapper.

5. In the search filtering path (SearchResults component), also filter and display browser tools. The SearchResults component currently shows 'Installed' plugins and 'Available' npm results. Browser tools should appear as part of the 'Installed' section, above the plugin list.

6. For active tool tracking: the MCP server sends tool.invocationStart/End with plugin='browser' for browser tools. The current activeTools Set uses keys like 'pluginName:toolName'. For browser tools the key would be 'browser:browser_list_tabs'. Check that the useServerNotifications hook correctly handles this — it probably already works since it constructs the key from the notification params.

Also edit platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/bridge.ts: update fetchConfigState or its return type to include browserTools from the response.

Also edit platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/SearchResults.tsx if needed to include browser tools in search results.

userStories.title: Add Storybook stories for BrowserToolsCard and updated SidePanel
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Storybook coverage for the browser tools section so I can visually verify all states.
acceptanceCriteria: New BrowserToolsCard.stories.tsx with stories: Default (all tools enabled), SomeDisabled, AllDisabled, WithActiveTool, WithToolFilter, Interactive (with toggle state)
SidePanel.stories.tsx updated with at least two new stories: WithBrowserTools (shows browser tools section above plugins), BrowserToolsOnly (connected but no plugins, only browser tools)
All stories render correctly (npm run storybook builds without errors)
userStories.notes:
Create platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/BrowserToolsCard.stories.tsx following the pattern of PluginCard.stories.tsx.

Create mock browser tool data. Use a subset of real browser tool names/descriptions for realism:

const mockBrowserTools: BrowserToolState[] = [
  { name: 'browser_list_tabs', description: 'List all open browser tabs', enabled: true },
  { name: 'browser_open_tab', description: 'Open a new browser tab with a URL', enabled: true },
  { name: 'browser_screenshot_tab', description: 'Capture a screenshot of a tab', enabled: true },
  { name: 'browser_click_element', description: 'Click an element matching a CSS selector', enabled: true },
  { name: 'browser_execute_script', description: 'Execute JavaScript in a tab', enabled: false },
  { name: 'extension_get_state', description: 'Get extension internal state', enabled: true },
];

Stories:
1. Default — all enabled, no filter, no active tools
2. SomeDisabled — execute_script disabled
3. AllDisabled — all enabled=false
4. WithActiveTool — activeTools has 'browser:browser_list_tabs'
5. WithToolFilter — toolFilter='screenshot'
6. Interactive — wrapper with useState for toggle

Update SidePanel.stories.tsx (same directory) to add:
1. WithBrowserTools — existing plugin stories data + browser tools data, showing both sections
2. BrowserToolsOnly — no plugins, only browser tools (tests the empty-plugins-but-has-browser-tools state — should NOT show NoPluginsState)

For the SidePanel stories, follow the existing SidePanelShell pattern used throughout that file. The shell replicates the App.tsx layout with SearchBar and Footer. Add browserTools prop and render BrowserToolsCard above the PluginList inside the shell.

Verify storybook builds: npx storybook build --config-dir platform/browser-extension/.storybook (or whatever the storybook config path is). Actually, just running npm run build should suffice for type checking — storybook build is separate.
